prompts.title: 5-Question Founder Voice Intake Doc
prompts.prompt: Act as a senior LinkedIn ghostwriter with 6 years experience writing for Indian B2B SaaS founders, VCs, and CXOs based out of Bengaluru, Mumbai, and Gurgaon. I need you to design a 5-question voice intake document I can send to a new founder client before our first writing sprint. Context I will share: founder name, company name, ARR band (sub ₹1cr, ₹1-10cr, Series A, Series B), city (HSR Layout, Indiranagar, Powai, etc.), education stamp (IIT/IIM/ISB or non-pedigree), and 3 LinkedIn posts they have written themselves. Please produce: (1) The 5 master questions phrased so a busy founder can voice-note answers in under 12 minutes total — covering origin story, three non-negotiable beliefs, three banned words/phrases, two heroes they will not name-drop publicly, and one topic they refuse to post about. (2) For each question, add a follow-up probe I can ask in the kickoff call to extract specifics. (3) A one-page "voice fingerprint" template I will fill in after the call — sentence length range, contractions yes/no, emoji budget per post, India references they lean into (UPI, ONDC, Aadhaar) and ones they avoid, and their default attitude toward Twitter/X founders, YC-Bengaluru scenes, and PE narratives. (4) A red-flag checklist of 6 founder behaviours that signal the engagement will fail (e.g., wants to sound like Naval, refuses to share losses, hates contrarian takes). Format as clean markdown sections so I can paste into Notion. Constraints: tone of the doc itself should sound like a senior operator, not a vendor. Default to Indian context — assume the founder reads Morning Context and The Ken, not TechCrunch.

prompts.title: Basic Story-Driven LinkedIn Post Template
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter who has written 4,000+ posts for Indian founders ranging from ₹1cr ARR seed-stage CEOs to Series B operators. I need a reusable story-driven post template I can fill in for any founder client in 20 minutes. Context I will provide: founder archetype (technical builder, sales-led, second-time founder, ex-consultant), one real moment from their week (a customer call, a hiring decision, a board prep, a Bengaluru office walk), and the single belief they want to land. Please produce: (1) A 5-block template — Hook (one line, scroll-stop on a feed full of YC-Bengaluru hot takes), Scene (3-4 lines, sensory detail like the HSR coffee shop or the WeWork Galaxy floor), Tension (the actual stakes — a ₹40L deal, a co-founder disagreement, a missed runway target), Insight (the founder's belief, said as them, not as a LinkedIn guru), CTA (a question that invites comments from other Indian founders, not generic engagement bait). (2) Three filled examples — one for a ₹2cr ARR vertical SaaS founder in Indiranagar, one for a Series A fintech CEO in Mumbai, one for a partner at a mid-size PE firm doing an RHIPL board appointment. (3) A 6-item self-edit checklist before publishing — banned phrases ("humbled to share", "thrilled to announce", "game-changer"), max 3 line breaks before the fold, max 1 emoji, no hashtags above 4. Format as markdown with clear labels. Constraints: every example must feel like an Indian founder wrote it on their phone between meetings — not a US thought-leadership post translated to INR.

prompts.title: 7-Day Content Plan for a New Founder Client
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter onboarding a new Indian B2B SaaS founder client (₹4cr ARR, post-seed, based out of HSR Layout, IIT-Madras alum, IIM-A MBA, building in the workflow automation space). I need a 7-day publishing plan for their first week on LinkedIn after a 6-month silent period. Context: they have 8,200 followers (mostly other founders, a few VCs from Blume, Stellaris, Peak XV), their goal is to be top-of-mind for a Series A raise in 5 months, and they can give me 25 minutes per day for inputs. Please produce: (1) A day-by-day calendar (Mon-Sun) with post type, hook angle, working title, target word count, posting time in IST, and the specific input I need from the founder for each post. (2) For each day, name the audience segment the post is targeting (peer founders, prospective hires, VCs, customers, ICP champions) and the single business outcome it serves. (3) A pre-week ritual — what to comment on, whose DMs to warm up, which 5 posts to engage with on Sunday night to prime the algorithm for the week. (4) A Friday review block — 4 metrics to check (impressions, profile visits, DMs from VCs, ICP comments) and what to do if any miss target. Format as a markdown table for the calendar plus prose for the rituals. Constraints: mix formats (1 story-driven, 1 contrarian, 1 customer quote-led, 1 behind-the-scenes, 1 carousel, 1 short opinion, 1 weekly recap). Default times must respect IST founder behaviour — assume best windows are 7:30-9:00 AM and 6:30-8:00 PM. Avoid generic "share your wins" advice.

prompts.title: Founder Archetype Matcher
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn strategist who has profiled 200+ Indian founders and execs by writing voice. I need a founder-archetype matcher that takes a 10-minute discovery call transcript and assigns the client to one of 8 archetypes I can write from confidently. Context I will share: a rough call transcript or 5 of their existing posts. Please produce: (1) The 8 archetypes with one-paragraph descriptions each — The Operator (ex-McKinsey, now ₹15cr ARR, prefers frameworks), The Builder (IIT, technical, posts about architecture decisions), The Storyteller (second-time founder, vulnerability-led), The Contrarian (ex-VC turned founder, hot takes on Indian SaaS), The Quiet Compounder (Series B CEO, low-volume, high-signal), The Network Node (solo VC or angel, posts to source deals), The Industry Sage (PE partner, RHIPL board appointee, writes like a sector report), The Emerging Voice (MBA student or junior PM transitioning to thought-leadership). (2) For each archetype, list 4 voice rules, 3 default formats, 5 banned moves, and 3 reference accounts (real Indian operators they should sound adjacent to, not copy). (3) A scoring rubric — 6 questions I can answer about a new client to land them in one archetype with a confidence score. (4) A blending rule for clients who span two archetypes (e.g., Builder + Contrarian). Format as markdown with clear archetype headers. Constraints: every reference account must be an Indian founder, VC, or operator — no Naval, no Paul Graham, no US Twitter analogues.

prompts.title: Banned-Word List Generator per Client
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter doing voice-fidelity QA for an Indian founder client. I need a custom banned-word list I can paste into my Grammarly or Notion doc before drafting any post. Context I will share: founder name, archetype (from the 8-archetype framework), 6 of their actual past posts, 3 posts they have explicitly said they hate, and the 2 LinkedIn voices they admire (must be Indian operators). Please produce: (1) A tiered banned list — Tier 1 (never use, instant kill), Tier 2 (use only if the founder's exact phrase from a Loom or call), Tier 3 (use sparingly with intent). Each tier should have at least 12 words/phrases. (2) For each banned word, give the reason (sounds like a US LinkedIn coach, sounds like an HR newsletter, sounds like a corporate press release, sounds like ChatGPT default) and 2 acceptable Indian-founder substitutes. (3) A separate "voice signature" list of 10 phrases this specific founder uses naturally that I should preserve and recycle (sourced from their existing posts and Loom transcripts). (4) Three structural bans — sentence patterns to avoid (e.g., "Here are 5 lessons I learnt", "Unpopular opinion:", "Hot take:"). (5) A monthly refresh ritual — how to update the list as the founder evolves. Format as a clean markdown checklist. Constraints: the list must reflect Indian founder Twitter/LinkedIn discourse, not US thought-leadership. Reference real terms like "flywheel", "moat", "GTM motion" only with caveats about overuse in the Indian SaaS feed.

prompts.title: Contrarian-Take Post Format
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter who has written 80+ contrarian-take posts that crossed 100k impressions for Indian founder clients. I need a structured contrarian-take format that lands without sounding like a Twitter ragebait account. Context I will share: founder client and archetype, the consensus belief in Indian SaaS or VC right now (e.g., "every D2C brand should go offline", "AI wrappers have no moat", "Bharat is the next big SaaS market"), and the founder's actual unconventional position with one piece of proprietary evidence (a customer data point, a hiring outcome, a churn number). Please produce: (1) A 6-block format — The Setup (state the consensus in one line as the loudest version of itself), The Tell (a single line that names who is saying this and why it has become consensus), The Break (the founder's contrarian position, said with calibration not bravado), The Evidence (the proprietary data, anonymised if needed), The Nuance (where the consensus is actually right — earns credibility), The Invitation (a question that pulls in disagreement from other Indian founders, not validation). (2) Three full-length drafts — one for a ₹6cr ARR vertical SaaS CEO, one for a Series B fintech founder, one for a Peak XV principal. (3) A risk-check — 5 questions to ask the founder before publishing (will this burn a customer, an investor, a co-founder, a hire, a portfolio company). Format as markdown. Constraints: no "unpopular opinion" opener, no "hot take" tag, no all-caps. Default to a Bengaluru-Mumbai SaaS audience. The post should make a Blume or Stellaris partner stop scrolling.

prompts.title: Learnings-from-Failure Post Builder
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter who specialises in vulnerability-led posts for Indian founders without making them sound performative. I need a learnings-from-failure post builder for a client who has just lost a key customer, missed a fundraise, fired a senior hire, or shipped a product that flopped. Context I will share: the actual failure (specific INR amount, headcount, timeline), the founder's archetype, what they actually learnt (not what they wish they had learnt), and the audience they are talking to (peer founders, future hires, customers, investors). Please produce: (1) A 5-block draft — The Concrete Failure (one line, no euphemism — "we lost a ₹1.2cr ARR customer last Tuesday"), The Backstory (3-4 lines on what they believed before), The Mistake (named clearly, no third-person framing), The Cost (financial, emotional, organisational — pick the most honest one), The Lesson (one line, transferable to other founders without becoming a LinkedIn aphorism). (2) A vulnerability calibration rubric — 4 dials I should set per client (specificity of numbers, naming people, timeline gap from event, emotional disclosure). (3) Three rewrite passes of the same failure for different archetypes (Operator, Builder, Quiet Compounder) showing how the same event lands differently. (4) A do-not-publish checklist — 6 signs the founder is processing in public, not teaching. Format as markdown. Constraints: no "failure is the best teacher" closing line, no humble brags disguised as failures, and never publish within 72 hours of the event itself. Indian founder context only — references to YC-Bengaluru scenes, Tracxn data, IndiaStack are fair game.

prompts.title: Customer-Quote-Led Post for B2B SaaS
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter who has written 120+ customer-quote-led posts for Indian B2B SaaS founders (₹2cr to ₹40cr ARR range). I need a post format that uses a real customer quote without sounding like a case study reshare. Context I will share: founder client, customer name (or anonymised role + industry), the verbatim quote from a sales call or NPS response, the underlying business outcome (a number, a switch from a competitor, a workflow that died), and what the founder wants the post to do (pull in similar ICPs, recruit a sales hire, attract investor attention). Please produce: (1) A 5-block format — The Setup (one line that frames the moment without revealing the quote), The Quote (verbatim, in italics, attributed precisely or anonymised cleanly — "a CFO at a ₹200cr revenue D2C brand told me last week"), The Context (why this customer was sceptical or what they had tried before — Zoho, Excel, an internal tool), The Outcome (the specific INR or hours-saved number), The Founder's Read (one line on what this signals about the market or the ICP). (2) Three drafts — one for a horizontal SaaS founder targeting Indian SMBs, one for a vertical SaaS CEO selling to BFSI, one for a developer-tools founder selling to engineering leaders at Indian unicorns. (3) A consent and accuracy checklist — 5 things to confirm with the customer before publishing. Format as markdown. Constraints: no fake-sounding quotes, no "this customer changed our business" framing. The quote must do the work; the founder is just curating.

prompts.title: Behind-the-Scenes Post for Series A Founders
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter who writes BTS posts for post-Series-A Indian founders that build trust without leaking confidential information. I need a behind-the-scenes post format that pulls back the curtain on one specific operating moment. Context I will share: founder, company stage (typically Series A or Series B, ₹10-50cr ARR), the specific moment to capture (a board prep at 11pm in the Indiranagar office, a sales QBR, a hiring debate between co-founders, a customer escalation call), what is shareable, and what is firmly off-limits (specific customer names, exact ARR, investor pushback details). Please produce: (1) A 5-block format — The Frame (time, place, who was in the room — sensory enough to feel real: HSR Social, WeWork Galaxy, the founder's home office at 6:45 AM), The Activity (what was actually happening — reviewing a pricing deck, debating a layoff, prepping for an RHIPL board meeting), The Tension (the unresolved question in the room, said abstractly enough to protect specifics), The Decision (what they chose, with reasoning), The Reflection (one line on what this taught the founder about scaling beyond ₹10cr ARR). (2) Three drafts at three sensitivity levels — high transparency, medium, low. (3) A confidentiality scrub — 6 specific things to redact before publishing (customer names, exact INR, hire names, investor language, competitor mentions, future product details). Format as markdown. Constraints: no "day in the life" framing, no LinkedIn-influencer cosplay. The post should make other Series A founders feel seen, not impressed.

prompts.title: 8 Hooks for One Idea
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter who tests hooks aggressively for Indian founder clients. I need a hook-generation prompt that takes one idea and produces 8 distinct hook options I can A/B mentally before shipping. Context I will share: the single idea (e.g., "why we stopped chasing Tier 1 enterprise logos at ₹3cr ARR"), founder archetype, and target audience segment for this specific post. Please produce: (1) 8 hooks across 8 distinct patterns — (a) Specific number + counter-expectation, (b) Named-customer-or-investor moment, (c) Direct contradiction of consensus, (d) Confession of a past mistake, (e) Question the audience is afraid to ask out loud, (f) Time-stamped scene ("Last Tuesday at 9:42 PM"), (g) Industry insider observation, (h) Single-word opener with a one-line follow. (2) For each hook, list the strongest archetype fit (which of the 8 founder archetypes it suits), the audience segment it pulls best (founders, VCs, hires, customers), and a feed-fit score (1-5) for the Indian B2B SaaS founder feed specifically. (3) The strongest 2 hooks rewritten in three lengths each (8 words, 14 words, 22 words). (4) A red-flag list — 5 hook patterns to retire forever ("Unpopular opinion:", "Here's what nobody tells you", "I built a ₹X company. Here's what I learnt:", "Story time:", "PSA:"). Format as markdown with clear hook numbering. Constraints: every hook must feel native to a Bengaluru founder feed. No US-coach-speak. No emoji-heavy openers.

prompts.title: Hook A/B Test Framework
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter running structured hook tests across a roster of 6-8 Indian founder clients. I need an A/B test framework I can run weekly to learn which hook patterns work for which founder type. Context I will share: my client roster (archetype + ARR band + audience), 4 weeks of post performance data (impressions, profile visits, DMs from ICP, comments from peers), and my current hook taxonomy. Please produce: (1) A weekly test design — 1 hook variable to isolate per client per week (opener pattern, line length, specific number presence, named entity, question vs statement), with a control hook to baseline against. (2) A measurement rubric — which 3 metrics matter for which founder goal (a Series A founder optimising for VC eyes vs a ₹2cr ARR founder optimising for ICP DMs vs a solo VC optimising for deal flow). (3) A minimum-sample rule — how many posts I need before declaring a winner, accounting for LinkedIn's noisy algo. (4) A 4-week rolling log template (markdown table) — date, client, hook pattern, hook text, impressions, ICP comments, DMs, win/lose verdict. (5) A quarterly synthesis prompt — how to compress 12 weeks of tests into 5 voice rules per client. (6) Three known anti-patterns I should stop testing because they consistently underperform on Indian founder feeds. Format as markdown. Constraints: do not over-trust impressions; weight ICP DMs and quality comments higher. Indian feed dynamics only — assume the algo behaves differently than US feeds for B2B content.

prompts.title: Scroll-Stopper for Indian Founder Feed
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter who has reverse-engineered why specific posts get 3x impressions on the Indian B2B founder feed (Bengaluru, Mumbai, Delhi NCR clusters). I need a scroll-stopper hook generator tuned to that feed specifically. Context I will share: the founder client, the topic of the post, and a screenshot or list of the last 10 posts in the founder's typical reader's feed (usually a mix of fundraise announcements, hiring posts, hot takes from VCs at Blume/Peak XV/Stellaris, and ONDC/UPI policy commentary). Please produce: (1) 6 scroll-stopper hooks engineered against the surrounding feed context — each one must visually and tonally break the pattern of the posts above and below it. (2) For each hook, name the specific feed-pattern it disrupts (e.g., breaks the "thrilled to announce" cluster, breaks the YC-Bengaluru hot-take wave, breaks the corporate press-release block). (3) A formatting guide — first 3 lines max length, line break placement before the "see more" cut, use of numerals over spelled numbers, capitalisation rules. (4) A timing layer — for each hook, recommend the best IST window (early-morning founder commute, lunch break, post-dinner scroll) given typical Indian founder phone-checking behaviour. (5) Two anti-hooks — what to never lead with on this feed (corporate-template openers, generic motivation, vague gratitude). Format as markdown. Constraints: every hook must be readable without the rest of the post. Treat the first 220 characters as the entire product.

prompts.title: 10-Slide Carousel Outline for SaaS Founder
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter who designs carousels that get saved (not just liked) by Indian B2B SaaS operators. I need a 10-slide carousel outline that walks through one substantive idea — not a listicle. Context I will share: founder client, archetype, the single idea (e.g., "how we cut CAC payback from 14 months to 6 months between ₹3cr and ₹8cr ARR"), and the audience the carousel must serve (peer founders, prospective hires, ICP buyers, or VCs prepping a meeting). Please produce: (1) A 10-slide structure — Slide 1 (cover hook + visual cue), Slide 2 (the stake — why this matters now), Slide 3 (the consensus approach), Slide 4 (why it failed for this founder), Slide 5 (the pivot moment with a concrete date or quarter), Slide 6 (the new framework in 3 parts), Slide 7 (the data — INR numbers, time-to-payback, headcount delta), Slide 8 (the trade-off they accepted — every honest framework has one), Slide 9 (one transferable principle), Slide 10 (CTA — DM, comment prompt, or link to a longer essay). (2) For each slide, the headline (max 8 words), 2-line body, and a visual brief for the designer (chart, screenshot, photo, illustration). (3) The accompanying caption (max 700 characters) that complements but does not duplicate the carousel. (4) A 4-item file-naming convention so the design hand-off is clean. Format as markdown. Constraints: carousel must work even with sound off, language off, and viewed on a mid-range Android phone. Default to Indian SaaS metrics norms (CAC payback, NRR, gross margin in INR).

prompts.title: Carousel Design Brief for Canva
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter who hands off carousel designs to a junior Canva designer in Bengaluru working on 4-hour turnaround. I need a design brief template that prevents 3 rounds of revisions. Context I will share: the 10-slide outline (from the carousel outline prompt), founder client's existing visual identity (brand colours, font if any, logo lockup, headshot availability), and where the carousel will live (LinkedIn only, or also repurposed to X and Instagram). Please produce: (1) A one-page brief covering — slide dimensions (1080x1350 native LinkedIn carousel), font hierarchy (H1, H2, body, caption with point sizes), colour palette (3 colours max — primary, accent, neutral), grid system, image treatment rules (full-bleed photo on cover, data viz on slides 4 and 7, headshot on slide 10). (2) A specific cover-slide rule — what makes an Indian founder carousel cover stop a thumb scrolling past 14 fundraise announcements. (3) Asset checklist — founder headshot (square crop, neutral background, max 800kb), company logo (SVG), data screenshots (anonymised), references to 3 carousels the designer should look at for style guidance (Indian operators only, name accounts that consistently ship clean carousels). (4) A do-not-do list — 6 visual moves that scream amateur (drop shadows, gradient text, stock photos of Western offices, generic icons from Flaticon, three-fonts-on-one-slide, emoji as bullets). (5) Revision protocol — what triggers v2 vs ship-as-is. Format as markdown. Constraints: assume designer has 90 minutes per carousel, not 6 hours. Brief must be skimmable in 4 minutes.

prompts.title: Hook-Cover-CTA Carousel Structure
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter who has analysed 300+ high-save carousels by Indian founders and operators. I need a tight Hook-Cover-CTA structural framework that ensures every carousel I ship has the three load-bearing slides done right. Context I will share: founder client, carousel topic, and the business outcome the carousel must drive (DMs from ICP, profile visits from VCs, applications for an open role, sign-ups for a webinar, comments from peer founders). Please produce: (1) The Hook Cover (Slide 1) framework — 4 cover patterns that work on the Indian founder feed (the named-number cover, the named-stakeholder cover, the contrarian-promise cover, the question-the-reader-fears cover) with one example each in the founder's voice. (2) The CTA Slide (Slide 10) framework — 5 CTA archetypes (DM trigger, comment-and-I-will-send, follow-for-part-2, link-in-bio essay, soft pitch) and which one matches which business outcome. (3) The bridge — why slide 9 is the most under-engineered slide in 90% of carousels and how to use it to set up the CTA without feeling salesy. (4) Three full Hook-Cover-CTA triplets for three founder archetypes (Operator, Storyteller, Network Node). (5) Anti-patterns — 5 cover slides and 5 CTA slides that consistently kill saves on Indian feeds. Format as markdown. Constraints: every CTA must respect the founder's positioning — a Series B CEO does not say "comment YES below". Reference real Indian founder behaviour, not US LinkedIn coach playbooks.

prompts.title: 1500-Word LinkedIn Article Outline
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter who writes long-form articles for Indian VCs and senior operators when they need to land a definitive POV the feed cannot carry. I need a 1500-word LinkedIn article outline. Context I will share: client (typically a partner at a PE firm, a Peak XV principal, a CXO at a Series C company, or an RHIPL board appointee), the central thesis (e.g., "why Indian B2B SaaS will need a new GTM playbook between $5M and $20M ARR"), the proprietary inputs they bring (deal data, portfolio observations, customer conversations), and the action they want the reader to take (book a meeting, share with their CFO, reach out for a board role). Please produce: (1) A 7-section outline — Title (max 70 characters, search-friendly), Standfirst (the 2-line opener that decides if the reader keeps going), Section 1 (the consensus to challenge), Section 2 (the proprietary evidence), Section 3 (the new framework in 3 named parts), Section 4 (the case study in INR specifics), Section 5 (the counter-argument the writer steelmans), Conclusion (the one-paragraph synthesis), Closing CTA. (2) For each section, target word count, the specific data point or anecdote it must contain, and the transition line into the next section. (3) An interview prompt list — 8 questions to ask the client to extract the article in a 45-minute Loom or call. (4) An accompanying LinkedIn post (140 words) that drives traffic to the article. Format as markdown. Constraints: avoid US-business-school framing. Default to Indian VC and operator vocabulary. The article must be the kind of thing a Blume or Stellaris partner would forward to their LP letter list.

prompts.title: Link-in-Bio Companion Strategy
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter who builds a link-in-bio system for Indian founders who want their LinkedIn presence to feed an owned channel (newsletter, podcast, hiring page, customer waitlist). I need a companion strategy that turns LinkedIn engagement into owned-audience growth. Context I will share: founder client, their owned destination (Substack, beehiiv, custom site, Notion landing page), current LinkedIn follower count, and the conversion event (newsletter sub, demo booking, hiring application, podcast download). Please produce: (1) A LinkedIn featured-section setup — 4 tiles to pin (lead magnet, latest essay, hiring page, podcast or talk) with copy and image specs. (2) A bio rewrite (max 220 characters in the about-line area visible above the fold) that names the client's POV and the one action a stranger should take. (3) An in-post linking pattern — how to mention the link without triggering LinkedIn's algorithmic penalty (first comment vs profile vs DM-on-keyword), with the trade-offs of each. (4) A monthly review — 3 metrics to track (LinkedIn-attributed newsletter subs, profile-to-link click rate, DM-keyword conversion rate). (5) A 4-post sequence template that warms a reader from cold-feed-impression to clicking a link without a single "link in bio" line that screams influencer. Format as markdown. Constraints: respect Indian founder taste — do not turn a Series B CEO into a creator-economy hustler. The system must look invisible.

prompts.title: Comment Template for VC Posts
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter who writes strategic comments under VC and senior operator posts to position founder clients without sounding like a sycophant. I need a comment template I can adapt in 90 seconds when a relevant VC posts something my client should be visible under. Context I will share: my founder client, the VC who posted (typically a partner or principal at Peak XV, Blume, Stellaris, Accel India, Lightspeed India, Z47, or a notable angel), the VC's post text, and the strategic objective (be top-of-mind for a future raise, build a relationship for a hiring intro, stake a public POV without DMing cold). Please produce: (1) A 4-line comment template — Acknowledge (one specific line about what the VC said, not generic praise), Add (one piece of proprietary signal from the founder's operating reality — a number, a pattern, a customer line), Anchor (one line of POV that is consistent with the founder's broader public position), Open (a question or invitation that gives the VC a reason to reply). (2) Four variations by VC type — partner posting a thesis, principal posting deal commentary, scout posting industry observation, ex-founder VC posting nostalgia. (3) A do-not-do list — 7 comment behaviours that hurt the founder's positioning ("Great post!", tagging others reflexively, contradicting the VC publicly, dropping the company name in the first line, asking for intros in the comment). (4) A weekly cadence — 5 comments per week is the right rate for a fundraising founder; 2 per week for a heads-down builder. Format as markdown. Constraints: the comment must be readable as the founder, not as a vendor.

prompts.title: DM-Pitch Script for Collab
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter who writes DM scripts that get replies from senior Indian operators (CXOs, VCs, founders, journalists). I need a DM-pitch script for collaboration outreach — guesting on a podcast, co-authoring a piece, joining a panel, sharing a customer reference. Context I will share: my founder client, the recipient (name, role, company, last public action — a post, a podcast, a fundraise announcement), the specific ask, and the relevance hook (mutual connection, shared portfolio, parallel customer base). Please produce: (1) A 4-paragraph DM script — Opener (one line referencing a specific recent action, not their bio), Bridge (why my client is reaching out now, with one credibility marker — current ARR, customer name, fund name), Ask (one clear, low-friction request — a 20-minute call, a written reply, a yes-or-no on an idea), Close (a graceful exit if it is not a fit). Total length max 110 words. (2) Three versions — warm (one mutual connection), lukewarm (no mutuals but recent public engagement), cold (no prior interaction). (3) Three reply-handlers — "yes let's chat" (next-step message), "maybe later" (nurture cadence), "not the right fit" (gracious close that preserves future option). (4) A no-go list — 6 DM patterns that get founder clients muted (voice notes from strangers, calendar links in the first message, generic praise, bulk-template tells, name-dropping without context). Format as markdown. Constraints: the DM must read like the founder typed it on their phone in the WeWork lift.

prompts.title: Monthly Recap Post to Network
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter who writes monthly recap posts for Indian founders and VCs that pull in DMs without sounding like a yearbook entry. I need a monthly recap format that turns 30 days of operating into 1 post that compounds the founder's network. Context I will share: founder client, the month's actual events (customer wins in INR, hiring closes, product ships, talks given, books read, conferences attended like SaaSBoomi, Nasscom, Mumbai Tech Week, YC-Bengaluru meetups, RHIPL board sessions), and the audience priority for next month (raising, hiring, selling, recruiting board members). Please produce: (1) A 6-block format — The Frame (one line that names the month's theme — not "crazy month" but a specific operating reality), Three Wins (each one line, with one specific number or named entity, balanced across customer/team/personal), One Loss (one line, named honestly), One Open Question (the founder's actual unresolved problem the network can help with), CTA (specific — "if you have hired your first VP Sales between ₹5cr and ₹15cr ARR I would love 20 minutes"). (2) Three drafts — one for a ₹3cr ARR founder, one for a Series B CXO, one for a solo VC. (3) A pre-publish gut check — 5 questions to ask before shipping (am I bragging, is the loss real, is the open question one the network can actually solve). (4) A network-warming sequence — what to do in the 48 hours after publishing to convert reads into 1:1 conversations. Format as markdown. Constraints: no "grateful" opener, no listing of every meeting taken. Indian context defaults — IST timezone, INR numbers, Indian conference references.

prompts.title: Positioning Statement for Solo VC
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter who positions solo VCs and emerging-fund GPs in the Indian early-stage ecosystem. I need a positioning statement I can use across the solo VC's LinkedIn headline, about section, pinned post, and DM signature. Context I will share: the VC's name, their cheque size in INR (typically ₹25L to ₹2cr), their stage focus (pre-seed, seed), their thesis areas (e.g., AI-native vertical SaaS, India-Bharat consumer, climate tech, dev tools), their unfair advantage (operator background, ex-founder, ex-VP at a unicorn, deep network in a niche), and the 3 founders they have already backed. Please produce: (1) A one-line positioning statement (max 22 words) usable on the LinkedIn headline that names the cheque, the stage, the thesis, and the operator edge. (2) An expanded 3-paragraph about-section rewrite (max 280 words total) that opens with their POV, names the 3 portfolio companies as evidence, and ends with the kind of founder they want to meet. (3) A pinned-post draft (max 220 words) — the manifesto post that anchors their feed. (4) A DM-signature line (max 18 words) for outbound to founders. (5) A differentiation audit — name 4 other Indian solo VCs in adjacent positioning and pinpoint how this one is non-substitutable. Format as markdown. Constraints: no "backing the next generation of Indian founders" cliche. Every line must do work.

prompts.title: Headline + About Rewrite for IIT/IIM Exec
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter rewriting headlines and about sections for senior Indian execs (VP, SVP, CXO at unicorns, partners at PE firms, MDs at consulting). I need a headline and about-section rewrite that respects credentialing without leading with it. Context I will share: exec's name, current role and company, 3 most recent roles, education (typically IIT/IIM/ISB/IIT-IIM combo), the audience they want to reach (board roles, advisory gigs, next CEO opportunity, consulting leads), and the POV they want to be known for. Please produce: (1) Three headline options (max 220 characters each) — one that leads with current role, one that leads with POV, one that leads with the outcome they enable. (2) An about-section rewrite (max 320 words) with 4 sections — Hook (the POV in 2 lines), Now (current role and what they are building), Track Record (3 named outcomes with INR/scale numbers), Open To (board, advisory, speaking, hiring). (3) A credentials-handling rule — when to lead with IIT/IIM and when to bury it. The default for Indian feeds is to bury it; surface it only when the audience is family-business or international. (4) Three rewrite traps to avoid — listing every employer, naming "thought leader" anywhere, using "passionate about". (5) A 4-week post sequence to bed in the new positioning. Format as markdown. Constraints: the about section must read as the exec, not as a recruiter's profile. Default Indian context — board appointments at RHIPL-style firms, panel slots at SaaSBoomi or Nasscom.

prompts.title: Work-History Reframe for Career Pivot
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter rewriting the work-history section for Indian execs and founders making a visible pivot — from operator to investor, from corporate to founder, from consulting to product, from MBA to thought-leadership. I need a work-history reframe that signals the new direction without erasing the old. Context I will share: client's actual roles for the last 10 years with company names, dates, and 1-line responsibilities, the pivot they are making, and the audience they need to convince (VCs, future co-founders, hiring managers in the new domain, MBA admissions). Please produce: (1) A reframe of each role's title (only where ethical — keeping factual accuracy) and 3-bullet description, leading with outcomes that are legible in the new domain. Each role should answer the implicit question: what does this prepare them for next. (2) A featured-experience block — 1 honorary entry (advisor role, board observership, side-project, fellowship like Antler, On Deck) that bridges old and new. (3) A skills-section reorder — top 5 skills to surface, bottom 5 to hide. (4) An education-section treatment — when to surface IIT/IIM honours and when to mute them so the new positioning leads. (5) A pivot-narrative paragraph (max 120 words) for the about section that explains the move without apologising for it. Format as markdown. Constraints: no inflation of past titles, no fabricated achievements. India context — pivots are typically read against MBA timelines, post-unicorn-exit norms, and family-business overhangs.

prompts.title: Founder Announcement Post
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter writing the founder-announcement post when an Indian operator goes public with their new company for the first time. I need an announcement post that does not sound like every "thrilled to announce" post on the feed. Context I will share: founder name, prior role (ex-VP at a unicorn, ex-partner at a VC, second-time founder), the new company name, what it does in one line, the stage of build (in-stealth, beta with 5 design partners, just raised pre-seed of ₹3cr from named angels), and the immediate ask (hires, design partners, intros). Please produce: (1) A 6-block draft — The Set (one line that names the move, no "thrilled"), The Why-Now (3 lines on the market shift that triggered the build — UPI 2.0, ONDC scale, GenAI margins, SaaS export wave), The What (1 line on the product, said as a founder, not as a marketer), The Who (the early team and 1-2 named angels or design partners), The Ask (specific — first 3 hires, first 5 design partners, first 2 advisors with a named profile), The Close (one line on what they will share next and when). (2) Three versions at three confidence levels — full disclosure, partial stealth, deep stealth. (3) A 7-day follow-up sequence — what to post next to keep the launch alive. (4) A pre-publish PR check — 5 things to coordinate with the angels, the previous employer, and any competitor watch. Format as markdown. Constraints: no fundraise theatre, no "on a mission to" framing. Default Indian context — Bengaluru/Mumbai launch norms, expectations from Inc42 / Entrackr coverage.

prompts.title: Hiring Post for Senior Roles
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter writing hiring posts for Indian B2B SaaS and consumer founders who need to attract a specific senior hire — VP Sales, Head of Engineering, first PM, founding designer, Head of Customer Success. I need a hiring post that gets 6-12 qualified inbounds, not 200 generic CVs. Context I will share: founder client, ARR band (₹3cr to ₹40cr), the role, the JD link, the comp band in INR, the location reality (Bengaluru in-office, Mumbai hybrid, fully remote), and the unfair-attraction levers the founder can pull (existing senior team, marquee customers, recent fundraise, specific tech stack). Please produce: (1) A 5-block hiring post — The Hook (one line that signals the calibre of operator wanted, e.g., "if you have scaled a B2B SaaS from ₹10cr to ₹50cr ARR with a sales team under 12, read this"), The Story (3 lines on the moment in the company that requires this hire — not generic growth, a specific operating problem), The Role (4 bullets — what they will own, who they will work with, the hard constraints, the trade-offs the founder is being honest about), The Comp (named band in INR, ESOPs framing, why the founder is sharing this), The Ask (DM with a specific 2-line answer, not a generic application link). (2) Three variations by archetype (Operator, Builder, Storyteller). (3) A 5-question DM screener the founder can paste back. Format as markdown. Constraints: no "rocketship" or "join the journey" language. Honest is the new edgy.

prompts.title: Fundraising Announcement Post
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter writing the fundraising announcement post for Indian founders raising rounds from pre-seed to Series B. I need a fundraising post that earns reshares from VCs and customers — not just polite likes. Context I will share: founder, company, round size in INR (or USD with INR equivalent), lead investor, participating investors and named angels, current ARR band, what the money is for in one line, and the previous round (if any). Please produce: (1) A 6-block draft — The Number (one line, named clearly — "we have raised ₹42cr Series A led by Peak XV"), The Why-Now (3 lines on what triggered the raise — a customer milestone, a product readiness, a hiring window — not vague "growth"), The Use (3 specific bullets in INR — sales hiring, engineering build-out, ICP expansion — no "category creation"), The Thanks (named investors, lead partner, board observer, with one specific line each on what each one brings — not generic gratitude), The Ask (specific — first 5 GTM hires, design partners in BFSI, intros to CFOs at ₹500cr+ companies), The Close (one line on what comes next). (2) Three sensitivity-tuned versions — full disclosure (round size, valuation hint, ARR), market-default (round size only), quiet (no number, just signal). (3) A 4-day follow-up sequence — what to post on Day 2, 3, 5, 7 to extend the announcement window. (4) Coordination checklist — what to align with the lead investor's communications team, Inc42, Entrackr, and the founder's WhatsApp groups before going live. Format as markdown. Constraints: no "humbled" opener. Indian VC ecosystem context only.

prompts.title: Layoff Communication Post
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter writing layoff-communication posts for Indian founders who need to speak publicly after letting people go. This is the highest-stakes post a founder will ever write. Context I will share: founder, company stage, headcount before and after, the actual reason (over-hired during 2021-22 boom, sales miss, runway extension, product pivot), what severance and outplacement support is being offered, and what the founder will say internally vs externally. Please produce: (1) A 5-block draft — The Acknowledgement (one line, no euphemism, no "hard decision" cliche — name what happened), The Reason (2-3 lines, owned by the founder, not blamed on the macro), The Support (specific — severance length in months, healthcare extension, outplacement intros, an open spreadsheet of impacted folks for hiring managers), The People (one line on what the impacted team built and what they should be hired for next), The Ask (a specific call to the network — hire these folks first, with a link). (2) A pre-publish protocol — what must happen internally before the post goes live (impacted team informed, remaining team briefed, board notified, key customers and investors warned), with a timeline. (3) A do-not-do list — 8 framings that have aged badly ("family" language, blaming "the funding winter", positioning the founder as the bigger victim, posting on the same day as the news, overpromising future rehires, sharing publicly without naming a single impacted person who consents). (4) A reply-management plan for the first 72 hours. Format as markdown. Constraints: this post is for the impacted team and their next employers — not for the founder's brand.

prompts.title: 30-Day Calendar for B2B SaaS Founder
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter building a 30-day publishing calendar for a Series A Indian B2B SaaS founder (₹12cr ARR, based in HSR Layout, IIT-Bombay alum, IIM-A MBA, selling to mid-market BFSI in India). I need a 30-day calendar that mixes formats and outcomes. Context I will share: business priorities for the quarter (close 3 enterprise logos, hire VP Sales, prep narrative for Series B in 6 months), founder's available time per week (90 minutes), and any planned external moments (a SaaSBoomi panel, an Inc42 podcast, a customer event in Mumbai). Please produce: (1) A 30-day calendar as a markdown table — Date, Day, Post Type (story-driven / contrarian / customer-quote / behind-the-scenes / carousel / article / recap), Working Title, Hook Angle, Audience Segment, Business Outcome, Founder Input Needed (Loom, voice note, written brief), Posting Window IST. (2) A weekly cadence — 3 posts per week is the right ship rate for this archetype; 4 in fundraise-prep weeks. (3) A monthly theme — pick one anchor narrative (e.g., "how mid-market BFSI buys software in India") that 60% of posts ladder up to. (4) Two reactive slots per week reserved for news-jacking ONDC/UPI/RBI moves. (5) A weekly review — 30-minute Friday sync agenda to triage performance and re-plan. Format as markdown. Constraints: no two consecutive posts in the same format. Mix vulnerability with authority. Default to Indian SaaS metrics norms in INR.

prompts.title: Monthly Theme for VC Client
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter running monthly thematic content for an Indian VC client (typically a partner or principal at Peak XV, Blume, Stellaris, Accel India, Lightspeed India, Z47, or a solo emerging-fund GP). I need a monthly thematic plan that turns the VC's deal flow and portfolio observations into 8-12 posts that ladder to a single thesis. Context I will share: the VC's stage focus, sector thesis areas, the 3-5 active deal threads they are pattern-matching across, and any portfolio milestones in the month (a portfolio Series B, a YC-Bengaluru cohort, an exit). Please produce: (1) A monthly theme statement (one line, max 16 words) that names the thesis the VC is making public this month. (2) An 8-post breakdown — 2 thesis posts, 2 portfolio-spotlight posts (with explicit portfolio company consent), 2 pattern-observation posts (anonymised deal flow learnings), 1 contrarian take, 1 monthly recap with named meetings and reads. (3) For each post, the hook angle, the data input needed from the VC, and the founder/company audience it pulls. (4) An LP-letter-friendly subset — which 3 posts of the 8 should be screenshotted and reused in the next quarterly LP update. (5) A network-warming layer — 12 strategic comments per month on founder posts that align with the thesis, with a target list of 25 founders. Format as markdown. Constraints: no "excited to back" announcements without a named thesis line. Indian early-stage context only.

prompts.title: Quarterly Content Review
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter running a quarterly review with a senior Indian founder or VC client. I need a structured quarterly content review framework that turns 90 days of posting into 5 decisions about the next quarter. Context I will share: the client, 90 days of post performance data (impressions, profile visits, ICP DMs, comments from peers, follower growth, link clicks where applicable), business outcomes attributed to LinkedIn (hires sourced, customer intros, investor meetings, board offers, podcast invites), and the client's revised quarterly business priorities. Please produce: (1) A 6-section review document — The Numbers (a table of the headline metrics with quarter-over-quarter delta), The Wins (the 3 posts that drove most ICP DMs and why), The Losses (the 3 posts that underperformed and what to retire), The Voice Drift (places the writing has drifted from the founder's true voice — caught in a Loom replay), The Audience Shift (who is following now vs 90 days ago and what it means), The Decisions (5 decisions for next quarter — format mix, theme, posting cadence, comment strategy, one experiment to run). (2) A 60-minute meeting agenda I can run with the client to walk through the review. (3) A re-contracting cue — 4 signals that the engagement should be renewed, repriced, or paused. (4) A handoff doc template if a junior writer is taking over execution. Format as markdown. Constraints: weight ICP DMs and quality comments above impressions. India SaaS / VC ecosystem context only.

prompts.title: DM Pitch to a CMO
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter writing DM pitches from an Indian B2B SaaS founder to a CMO at a target enterprise account (large Indian company — Asian Paints, Tata Digital, HDFC Bank, Marico, ITC, or a unicorn like Razorpay, Meesho, Cred). I need a DM that opens a sales conversation without triggering the gatekeeper-block reflex. Context I will share: founder client, product (one line), current customer base (named logos if shareable), the specific reason this CMO matters now (recent press, hiring, public talk, product launch, panel appearance), the warm intro path if any, and the meeting outcome wanted (15-min discovery, async memo response, intro to the right team member). Please produce: (1) A 4-paragraph DM (max 130 words total) — Opener (referencing one specific recent action by the CMO, with a sentence that proves the founder actually engaged with it, not skimmed the headline), Bridge (one line on why the founder's product is relevant to the CMO's current public priority), Proof (one customer-shaped sentence — "we just shipped this for [named or anonymised peer]"), Ask (one specific micro-ask — 15 minutes, an async memo, an intro). (2) Three variants — warm-intro version, cold-with-mutuals version, fully-cold version. (3) A follow-up cadence — message 2 at day +5, message 3 at day +14, then a graceful retirement. (4) A red-flag list — 6 patterns that get founder DMs reported (immediate calendar links, voice notes, pitch decks attached unsolicited, bulk-template tells, asking the CMO to forward to someone else, tagging the company in the first message). Format as markdown. Constraints: must read like a peer reaching out, not a vendor.

prompts.title: Comment-Stalker Analysis of a Founder's Network
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter doing a network-mapping exercise for an Indian founder client who wants to know who is actually engaging with them, not just who follows. I need a comment-stalker analysis framework — a structured way to mine the comments and reactions on the last 60 days of the founder's posts and turn them into a relationship action list. Context I will share: founder client, an export or screenshot of comment and reactor data from the last 12 posts, and the business priority the relationships should serve (raise, hire, sell, recruit board members). Please produce: (1) A tagging schema — categorise every commenter into 6 segments (Peer Founder, Investor, Customer Champion, Prospective Hire, Industry Voice, Noise) with rules for ambiguous cases. (2) A signal-extraction layer — for each commenter, score 3 dimensions (relevance to current priority, frequency of engagement, depth of comment vs emoji-reaction). (3) A 4-tier action list — Tier 1 (5-7 names to DM with a specific ask in the next 7 days), Tier 2 (10-15 names to nurture with a personal comment back this week), Tier 3 (20-30 names to keep visible to via consistent posting), Tier 4 (mute / no action). (4) A recurring monthly cadence — how to refresh the analysis without spending more than 90 minutes per month. (5) A privacy and ethics note — what is fair game from public engagement vs what crosses into surveillance. Format as markdown. Constraints: respect Indian founder network norms — no scraping, no third-party tools that violate LinkedIn ToS.

prompts.title: India SaaS Conference Recap Post
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter writing post-event recap content from Indian SaaS and startup conferences — SaaSBoomi (Chennai), Nasscom Product Conclave (Bengaluru), Mumbai Tech Week, YC-Bengaluru meetups, RHIPL annual, Surge demo days, Peak XV portfolio offsites. I need a recap post format that earns reshares from other attendees and signals presence to those who could not attend. Context I will share: client, the event, the 3-4 sessions or conversations that mattered most to the client, named operators/investors they spent time with (with consent), and the one POV they want the recap to land. Please produce: (1) A 5-block recap draft — The Frame (one line that names the event and the angle the founder is taking — not "another great event"), Three Specific Takeaways (each tied to a named session, panellist, or 1:1 conversation, with one concrete number or quote per takeaway), One Counter-Observation (where the consensus at the event was wrong or incomplete), The People (1-2 named operators the founder learnt from, with one specific line each), The Forward (what the founder is doing differently in the next 30 days as a result). (2) Three formats — short post (200 words), carousel outline (6 slides), long-form recap (700 words) for clients who want depth. (3) A pre-event prep — what to capture during the event (voice notes, photos, names, quotes) so the recap writes itself in 45 minutes. (4) A 24-hour rule — why the recap must ship within a day of the event closing. Format as markdown. Constraints: no "thank you to the organisers" line. India SaaS context defaults — recognisable speaker names, real session formats.

prompts.title: Monthly Newsletter Outline
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter who writes the monthly LinkedIn Newsletter for Indian founders, VCs, and senior operators. I need a monthly newsletter outline that fits LinkedIn's native newsletter format and gets resubscribes. Context I will share: client, their LinkedIn follower count, the newsletter's central thesis (e.g., "how Indian B2B SaaS companies are getting to ₹100cr ARR with sub-50 person teams"), the client's available time per month (3 hours), and the 3 ongoing themes they are tracking. Please produce: (1) A 6-section newsletter outline — Title (max 60 characters, search-friendly), Subtitle (one line that frames the issue), Editor's Note (120 words, written as the client, framing the month), The Main Idea (700 words on one substantive observation, with 2 named examples in INR), The Pattern (250 words on a recurring signal across 3 conversations or deals this month), The Reads (4 links — 2 to other operators' work, 1 to a podcast, 1 to a piece of data the client found surprising), The Open Question (the client's actual unresolved problem the readership can help with). (2) An interview prompt list — 6 questions to ask the client in a 40-minute monthly call to extract the entire issue. (3) A LinkedIn post (140 words) that promotes the newsletter without sounding spammy. (4) A subscriber-growth tactic per issue — one specific move (a guest contribution, a co-publish with another newsletter, a comment campaign on a related VC's post) to grow subs by 100-300 in 14 days. Format as markdown. Constraints: no listicles, no "5 lessons from" framing. Default Indian operator context.

prompts.title: PR-Tied LinkedIn Post for Funding Announcement
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter coordinating LinkedIn announcement content with PR coverage for an Indian startup's funding round. I need a LinkedIn post sequence that complements (not duplicates) the Inc42 / Entrackr / YourStory / Moneycontrol coverage. Context I will share: founder, round size in INR, lead investor, PR embargo lift time in IST, the journalist's angle in each publication, and the LinkedIn-only colour the founder wants to add (the operating reality the press release glossed over). Please produce: (1) A 4-post sequence — Post 1 (T+0, the founder's announcement that adds proprietary detail the press did not cover — a customer milestone, a hiring story, a why-now nobody asked about), Post 2 (T+1 day, a thank-you-to-named-people post that gives credit to early angels, design partners, first hires by name with consent), Post 3 (T+3 days, a behind-the-scenes post on what closing the round actually looked like — the 4-month diligence, the term-sheet pivot, the 11 PM Indiranagar coffee), Post 4 (T+7 days, the forward-looking ICP/hiring post that converts the announcement attention into pipeline). (2) For each post, the hook, the body structure, and the input needed from the founder. (3) A PR-coordination checklist — 6 alignments with the lead investor's comms team, the in-house comms, and the founder's WhatsApp groups before T+0. (4) A reply-management protocol for the first 72 hours — which DMs to triage to the founder and which to handle as the ghostwriter. Format as markdown. Constraints: no duplicating the press release verbatim. The LinkedIn arc must add what only the founder can say.

prompts.title: MBA-to-Thought-Leadership Transition Plan
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter onboarding a final-year IIM-A or ISB MBA student who wants to transition to a public thought-leadership presence before they graduate and join their PE / consulting / startup role. I need a 90-day positioning and content plan. Context I will share: student name, school, work background pre-MBA (typically 3-5 years at McKinsey/BCG, an Indian unicorn, or a family business), post-MBA role (PE associate at a mid-cap PE firm, consulting senior associate, founder's office at a Series B startup), the specific niche they can credibly own, and current LinkedIn footprint. Please produce: (1) A positioning statement they can use across their headline and about section that bridges student-now and operator-soon. (2) A 90-day content arc in 3 phases — Phase 1 (Days 1-30, build credibility through deeply-researched 3 posts a week on the chosen niche), Phase 2 (Days 31-60, layer in named opinion and original frameworks, 2 posts plus 1 carousel a week), Phase 3 (Days 61-90, transition into operating-voice content tied to the new role, 2 posts plus a monthly newsletter issue). (3) A peer reference set — 5 Indian operators in the niche they should read religiously without copying. (4) A no-go list — 6 patterns that brand them as "another MBA on LinkedIn" (frameworks-for-everything, name-dropping the school in every post, posing as a senior operator, hot takes without operating evidence, generic motivation, networking-event recap posts). (5) A measurement layer — 3 metrics that matter at this stage (profile views by target firms, DMs from operators 5+ years senior, invites to write or speak). Format as markdown. Constraints: respect the credibility ceiling of being pre-graduation. Build slow.

prompts.title: Loom-to-Post Translation Workflow
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter who runs an asynchronous workflow with senior Indian founder clients who refuse to do interview calls. I need a Loom-to-post translation workflow — a system that turns a 6-minute Loom from the founder into a publish-ready post with one revision cycle. Context I will share: client, archetype, voice doc, banned-word list, and a Loom transcript (or the Loom itself). Please produce: (1) A 5-step workflow — Step 1 (Loom prompt I send the founder — a 4-line message with the question, a single example of what good looks like, and a max-time budget), Step 2 (transcript clean-up rules — what to keep verbatim, what to compress, what to cut), Step 3 (post draft using the voice doc and banned-word list, max 220 words for short posts, max 380 for long), Step 4 (a 6-line rationale memo for the founder explaining 3 choices I made and 1 question I need them to answer), Step 5 (publish-ready file with caption, hashtags max 4, image brief, and posting time recommendation). (2) Three Loom prompts I can rotate — "tell me about a moment this week", "what is the contrarian take you have but have not posted", "who is wrong about [topic] in Indian SaaS right now". (3) A 24-hour SLA for first draft from Loom receipt. (4) A failure mode catalogue — 5 things that break this workflow (founder gives 22-minute Loom, founder rambles without specifics, founder talks in third person, founder mentions an unsigned customer, founder forgets the audience). Format as markdown. Constraints: zero in-person calls. Workflow must scale to 6-8 clients without dropping quality.

prompts.title: UPI / ONDC / Aadhaar News-Jacking Post
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter who jacks India-stack news cycles for founder clients without sounding like a policy commentator who has never built anything. I need a news-jacking post format for moves in UPI, ONDC, Aadhaar, OPEN banking, account aggregator framework, RBI digital lending guidelines, and SEBI fintech rulings. Context I will share: founder client, the specific news event (e.g., NPCI announces UPI Lite limit increase, ONDC TSP onboarding rule change, RBI tokenisation deadline), the date the news broke, and the founder's actual operating angle (a customer affected, a product change forced, a thesis confirmed or broken). Please produce: (1) A 5-block draft — The News (one line, factual, no journalism cosplay), The Operating Reality (3 lines on what this means for the founder's specific business or customer base — INR impact, timeline, headcount), The Pattern (one line that connects this to a longer arc the founder has been writing about), The Take (the founder's POV, said with calibration, not bravado), The Question (a specific question that pulls in other operators dealing with the same change). (2) Three timing rules — when to post within 24 hours, when to wait 48 hours for nuance, when not to post at all. (3) A credibility check — 5 questions to ask before posting (do I have skin in the game on this, am I adding signal or just amplifying, will this age well in 6 months, am I commenting outside my zone, would the founder say this in a closed founder WhatsApp group). (4) Two anti-patterns — the policy-explainer post (boring, low signal) and the rage-tweet (low credibility). Format as markdown. Constraints: India-stack context is the default, not a sidebar.

prompts.title: Board Appointment Announcement
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter writing the board-appointment announcement for an Indian senior operator joining the board of a listed Indian company (RHIPL-style appointment), a unicorn, a portfolio company, or a non-profit. I need a post that signals seriousness without slipping into corporate boilerplate. Context I will share: client, current role, the company they are joining the board of, the nature of the seat (independent director, observer, advisory board member), what they bring (operating experience, sector depth, specific function — finance, product, regulatory), and any disclosure constraints from the company secretary. Please produce: (1) A 4-block post — The Acknowledgement (one line, specific — "I have joined the board of [Company] as an independent director, effective [date]"), The Why (3 lines on why this seat — what the company is doing that the client wants to be inside the room for), The Bring (3 lines on what the client is bringing — said as the operator, not the resume), The Forward (one line on what they expect to learn). (2) A disclosure checklist — 6 items to clear with the company secretary before posting (regulatory disclosures, embargoes, compensation references, mentions of co-directors, mention of the search firm, photo permissions). (3) A 4-week post-appointment cadence — what to post next that signals engagement without leaking board-room content. (4) A do-not-do list — 7 framings that signal naivete ("honoured", "humbled", over-thanking the chair, naming search firm without permission, posting before the company's own announcement). Format as markdown. Constraints: this post is read by the company's other directors and shareholders. Tone must match.

prompts.title: Customer-Advocacy Re-Share Post
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter writing the post that re-shares or quote-amplifies a customer's organic mention of the founder's company. I need a re-share format that compounds the social proof without making the founder look thirsty. Context I will share: founder client, the customer's original post (text and link), the relationship (warm customer, advocate, named user), the customer's seniority (founder, CXO, VP, IC), and any consent the customer has given. Please produce: (1) A 4-block re-share — The Setup (one line that names the moment without quoting the customer yet — "a CFO at a ₹120cr revenue D2C brand posted something on Friday that I want to add context to"), The Quote (a precise pull-out of 1-2 lines from the customer's post, in italics, attributed by role and industry, with link), The Story Behind (3 lines on the operating reality the customer is referring to — the actual problem solved, the trade-off the team made, the months it took), The Acknowledgement (one line of credit to the customer's team, named only with consent). (2) A consent script — a one-paragraph DM the founder can send the customer to ask for re-share permission and tagging. (3) A timing rule — re-share within 36 hours of the customer's post or wait 7 days; never the same day to avoid looking coordinated. (4) A do-not-do list — 6 patterns that hurt (re-sharing without crediting context, adding sales CTAs, tagging the customer's competitors, claiming credit for the customer's outcome, posting the customer's logo without brand-team approval). Format as markdown. Constraints: the customer remains the main character.

prompts.title: Talk / Podcast Promotion Post
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter writing the post that promotes a founder client's appearance on a talk, panel, or podcast (SaaSBoomi keynote, The Pragmati podcast, The Neon Show, Inc42 video series, a Nasscom panel). I need a promotion post format that pulls listens, not just likes. Context I will share: founder, the show or stage, the host or panel, the specific topic, the publish or event date, the link, and the 2-3 ideas from the conversation that are most counter-intuitive. Please produce: (1) Two post variants — Pre-Drop (3 days before, builds anticipation by previewing the most contrarian idea, no link, ends with "link drops on [date]") and Drop-Day (the day the episode goes live, leads with the single most non-obvious idea, includes the link in the first comment, names the host with credit). (2) A 5-block structure for the Drop-Day post — Hook (one line that previews the contrarian idea, not "new episode out"), Context (2 lines on why this conversation now), Three Pull-Quotes (each 1-2 lines, in italics, surfacing the most useful ideas), The Credit (one line naming the host and what they did well as an interviewer), The CTA (link in first comment, plus a question that pulls in listener replies). (3) A 7-day amplification plan — what to post on day 2, 4, 7 to keep the episode alive. (4) A do-not-do list — 5 patterns that kill listens ("check out my latest podcast", listing every topic the conversation covered, generic gratitude to the host, dropping link in the post body which depresses reach, no preview of substance). Format as markdown. Constraints: assume the reader has not watched a podcast in 3 weeks.

prompts.title: Reply-Block Plan for High-Engagement Posts
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter handling reply management on a high-engagement post for an Indian founder client (a fundraise announcement, a layoff post, a contrarian take that crossed 500k impressions, a hiring post that pulled 200+ DMs). I need a structured reply-block plan I can execute in 90 minutes per day for the first 5 days. Context I will share: founder, the post, the reply volume in the first 12 hours, the DM volume, the audience priority, and how much of the response the founder will personally handle vs delegate to me. Please produce: (1) A triage rubric — categorise every comment and DM into 5 buckets (High-Value Engage — VCs, named operators, target customers; Warm Acknowledge — peer founders, hires, advocates; Generic Like-Back — supportive but low-signal; Ignore — spam, sycophancy, irrelevant; Escalate to Founder — sensitive, opportunity, risk). (2) Reply templates for each bucket, written in the founder's voice — 5 variations each so they do not look templated. (3) A DM-to-meeting conversion playbook — for High-Value DMs, the 3-message arc that moves from intro to a 20-minute call. (4) A 5-day cadence — how time should be split across day 1 (60% of effort), day 2 (20%), day 3-5 (taper to nurture). (5) A founder-handoff list — which 8-12 messages must reach the founder's eyes daily, and how to summarise them in a 4-line digest. Format as markdown. Constraints: zero auto-likes from the founder account, zero copy-paste tells. Indian network context — respect WhatsApp follow-up culture.

prompts.title: Founder vs Company-Page Content Split
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter advising an Indian B2B SaaS founder on what to post from their personal handle vs the company page. I need a split rubric and a monthly content allocation plan. Context I will share: founder, company stage (₹3cr to ₹40cr ARR), current company page follower count, current founder follower count, the in-house marketing team (or absence of one), and the business priorities of the quarter. Please produce: (1) A 6-axis split rubric — Storytelling (founder), Product Updates (company), Customer Wins (split — founder for the human story, company for the case study), Hiring (founder for senior, company for IC), Fundraising (founder lead, company echo), Industry Commentary (founder only). For each axis, 2 lines on why and 1 example. (2) A monthly allocation — for a typical Series A founder, 12 founder posts and 8 company posts is the right ratio; explain how to flex this in fundraise prep, hiring sprints, and product launch weeks. (3) A reuse rule — when to repost a founder post from the company page (almost never, with 3 specific exceptions). (4) A voice-divergence rule — how the company page tone should sit one notch more institutional than the founder's, with examples of the same announcement said both ways. (5) A measurement layer — separate KPIs for the two handles (founder optimises for ICP DMs and follower quality, company optimises for site traffic and demo bookings). Format as markdown. Constraints: assume the founder is the company's largest distribution channel for the first 4 years.

prompts.title: Anniversary / Milestone Post Without Cringe
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter writing milestone posts for Indian founders — 1-year work anniversary at the company they founded, 100th customer, ₹10cr ARR crossing, 50th hire, first international customer, first profitable month. I need a milestone post format that does not slip into the "thank you for the journey" trap. Context I will share: founder, the specific milestone, the actual numbers, the time it took (in months or years), and the moment that made the milestone visible (an internal email, a customer call, a board update). Please produce: (1) A 5-block draft — The Number (one line, named precisely with date — "yesterday we crossed 100 customers, 38 months after the first one signed"), The Stretch (3 lines that reset reader expectation — what 100 customers actually means at this ARR band, what is misleading about the number, what the team would have wanted to be true 38 months ago), The Moment (the small operating moment that made the milestone real — not a champagne-bottle photo), The People (named, with consent, with one specific line each — co-founders, first hires, first customers, first investors), The Forward (one line — what the next milestone is and why this one does not change the day-to-day). (2) Three sensitivity-tuned versions — high transparency (numbers and INR), medium (named milestone, no INR), quiet (signal without numbers). (3) A no-go list — 6 patterns that age badly ("never imagined we would be here", listing every team member, founder selfies, generic gratitude, comparison to other startups, premature victory laps). Format as markdown. Constraints: the milestone is for the team, not the founder's personal brand.

prompts.title: Cold-Open Post for Re-Engaging After a Silent Period
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter writing the first post for an Indian founder client who has been silent on LinkedIn for 3-12 months. I need a cold-open post format that does not start with an apology. Context I will share: founder, the silence length, what they were doing during the silence (heads-down product build, fundraise prep, personal sabbatical, post-layoff recovery, post-acquisition transition), and the specific reason they are coming back now. Please produce: (1) A 4-block draft — The Quiet (one line that names the silence factually — "I have not posted in 7 months" — without making it the headline), The Substance (3-4 lines that name the actual operating reality of those 7 months — what they built, what they learnt, what they killed — with one number), The Forward (3 lines on what they will be writing about for the next quarter, with a named theme), The Invitation (a specific ask of the readership — design partners, hires, peer founders to compare notes with). (2) Three openings to pick from depending on the silence reason — the build-mode return, the post-fundraise return, the post-layoff return. (3) A 14-day re-warm sequence — 5 posts in the next two weeks that build on the cold-open without forcing the founder back into a heavy cadence. (4) A no-go list — 6 patterns that signal the founder is uncertain about being back ("I am bad at LinkedIn", apologising, listing reasons for the absence as personal struggle without specificity, performative humility, leading with a hot take to seem confident). Format as markdown. Constraints: cold-open must not feel like the founder is restarting from zero. They are picking up where they left off, with new substance.

prompts.title: Pricing-and-Scope Doc for Ghostwriting Engagement
prompts.prompt: Act as a senior LinkedIn ghostwriter pricing and scoping a new engagement with an Indian founder or VC client. I need a pricing-and-scope document I can send within 24 hours of a discovery call. Context I will share: client name, archetype, current LinkedIn footprint, business outcomes they want LinkedIn to drive in the next 6 months, time they will commit per week (Loom and review), and any prior ghostwriting engagements they have run. Please produce: (1) A 3-tier scope — Tier 1 (Foundation — voice doc, 8 posts a month, monthly review, ₹X INR), Tier 2 (Growth — voice doc, 12 posts a month including 2 carousels and 1 long-form, comment strategy, monthly review, ₹Y INR), Tier 3 (Operator — Tier 2 plus monthly newsletter, DM-pitch scripts, reply management on high-engagement posts, quarterly review, ₹Z INR). Use realistic INR ranges for the Indian ghostwriting market in 2026 (Tier 1 ₹60k-₹1.2L per month, Tier 2 ₹1.2L-₹2.5L, Tier 3 ₹2.5L-₹5L). (2) A scope-exclusion list — 8 things explicitly out of scope (company-page content, paid ads, video editing, podcast production, HR comms, crisis comms, press release distribution, design beyond a Canva carousel hand-off). (3) A success-criteria block — 4 measurable outcomes per tier the engagement will be judged against in the 90-day review. (4) A kill-clause — the conditions under which either side can exit cleanly. (5) A onboarding sequence — week 1 deliverables, week 2-4 ramp. Format as markdown. Constraints: pricing must reflect senior India market rates, not US rates. Scope must be defendable to a CFO.

prompts.title: Founder Q&A Post for Recruitment
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter writing Q&A-style founder posts that double as recruitment magnets — the founder answers 5-7 questions a senior candidate would ask in the first interview, posted publicly. I need a Q&A post format. Context I will share: founder, company stage, the open role this is targeting (typically a senior IC or first-line manager), and the 5-7 questions the founder consistently gets asked by candidates (about ESOPs in INR, runway, tech stack, hybrid policy for Bengaluru and remote, what success looks like in the role, what the founder will do that they will hate, what the founder won't do that they will need). Please produce: (1) A 3-block format — The Frame (one line on why the founder is publishing this — "5 questions every senior PM candidate has asked me in the last 4 weeks"), The Q&A (5-7 questions with honest answers in 2-3 lines each, written as the founder, not as a hiring page), The Ask (a specific DM ask with a 2-line filter — "if these answers do not put you off, DM me with one example of [specific thing]"). (2) A draft for a 1st-PM-hire post and a draft for a Head-of-Engineering hire post, both in a Series A founder voice. (3) An honesty calibration — for each question, what the founder must not over-promise (ESOP liquidity timeline, hybrid flex, runway certainty, founder availability). (4) A reply-management plan — how to respond to candidates who DM in the first 72 hours. Format as markdown. Constraints: the post must read like the founder talking, not the people-team writing. Indian comp norms in INR. Honest beats polished.

prompts.title: Crisis-Response Post for Public Misstep
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter writing the crisis-response post for an Indian founder or company after a public misstep — a product outage, a data breach, a tone-deaf marketing campaign, an employee complaint that went viral, a co-founder departure, a customer escalation that hit Twitter. I need a crisis-response post structure that earns trust back without slipping into PR-speak. Context I will share: client, the misstep (what happened, when, who is affected, what is verifiably true vs still under investigation), the 24-72 hour window we are responding in, the legal constraints, and what the founder is willing to commit to publicly. Please produce: (1) A 5-block draft — The Acknowledgement (one line, factual, no euphemism, no "we are aware" deflection), The Impact (2-3 lines naming who is affected and how, in specifics — number of customers, types of data, hours of downtime), The Cause (1-2 lines on what went wrong, owned by the founder, not blamed on a vendor or junior team member), The Action (4 specific commitments with timelines — what is being fixed, by when, how customers will be informed, what the company will do differently structurally), The Channel (one line on where customers should go for ongoing updates and reach the founder directly). (2) A pre-publish protocol — 6 alignments with legal, security, customer success, and the board before the post goes live. (3) A 3-day follow-up cadence — what to post next as the situation evolves. (4) A do-not-do list — 8 framings that compound the crisis (defensive language, blaming users, premature "resolved", over-apology theatre, founder selfies, posting on the same day as the news without verified facts). Format as markdown. Constraints: legal-safe, customer-first, founder-honest. Indian regulatory context — DPDP Act, RBI guidelines, SEBI disclosures where relevant.

prompts.title: Investor-Update LinkedIn Post (Selective Public Update)
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter writing a selective public investor-update post for Indian founders who want to share quarterly progress with a wider network without giving away proprietary numbers. I need a public-update format that signals momentum to angels, future investors, and prospective hires while keeping competitive intelligence safe. Context I will share: founder, last raise context (round size, investors, runway), what is shareable from the quarter (broad ARR band growth, named hires, named customer wins with consent, product launches), what is firmly off-limits (exact ARR, exact runway, exact churn, customer-specific revenue, competitive positioning), and the specific audience pull (angels for follow-on, hires for two open roles, design partners for a new product line). Please produce: (1) A 6-block draft — The Frame (one line that names the period — "a public version of the Q1 FY26 investor update I sent last week"), Three Operating Wins (each 1-2 lines, with one number that is impressive but not competitively sensitive — e.g., "crossed our highest gross margin month" instead of exact GM%), One Honest Loss (one line, named, owned), The People (2-3 named hires or design partners with one line each), The Asks (specific — 2 hires, 3 design partner profiles, 1 advisor profile, said precisely). (2) A redaction checklist — 8 things to scrub before publishing (exact INR ARR, exact churn, exact CAC, named churned customers, investor pushback, runway in months, competitor names, future product timelines). (3) A timing rule — post within 14 days of the private investor update going out, never on the same day. Format as markdown. Constraints: signal without specificity. Treat the post like a redacted version of the cap-table update.

prompts.title: Year-End Letter for Indian Founder or VC
prompts.prompt: Act as a LinkedIn ghostwriter writing the year-end letter post for an Indian founder, VC, or senior operator — the late-December piece that wraps the year and sets up the next one. This is the post that signals taste, judgement, and trajectory. I need a year-end letter format that earns reshares from peers and does not blur into the wall of identical "2026 wrap" posts. Context I will share: client, the year's actual events (named milestones with INR and timelines, named losses, named hires and exits, named board appointments, named talks and writing), the themes they have been pattern-matching across portfolios or operating reality, the questions they have not yet answered, and the explicit positioning they want to land for the new year. Please produce: (1) A 6-block draft (target 450-600 words for the post) — The Frame (one line that names the year's central operating reality without using "crazy" or "wild"), Three Things That Surprised Me (each 2-3 lines, with named specifics — a customer behaviour, a hire that worked, a thesis that broke, an India-stack development like UPI international rollout or ONDC TSP scale), Three Things I Was Wrong About (each 2-3 lines, owned, with the corrected take), Three People Who Shaped My Year (named with consent, with one specific line on what each one taught), The Question I Am Carrying Into Next Year (one paragraph on the genuine open problem the client is still working through), The Forward (3 lines on what they will build, write, or back in the new year). (2) An accompanying carousel outline (8 slides) that compresses the letter for the scroll-only audience. (3) A timing rule — publish between Dec 22 and Dec 31, in IST, optimised for the 9-10 PM founder reading window after the workday but before NYE. (4) A no-go list — 7 framings that age badly ("grateful", listing every meeting taken, performative humility, vague predictions for the new year, generic life lessons, name-drops without context, photos of NYE celebrations). Format as markdown. Constraints: this is a load-bearing post for the client's brand for the next 90 days. Every paragraph must do work.
